narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, LSAT score of 27, Year 1 school decile of 2, Year 3 decile of 1, upper-middle family income, and full-time student status—and predicted a 63.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is well below the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile and signaled to the model a significant drop-off in performance relative to peers. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 2, also lower than the average of about the 6th decile, reinforcing concerns about your early academic standing compared to others in the dataset. The third key factor was your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction slightly toward passing, as it was closer to passing patterns seen in successful students despite being below the dataset average of 3.21. Overall, your decile rankings stood out as unusually low against typical applicants, outweighing positives like your GPA and common full-time status that 93% of students share. While your upper-middle family income aligns with many in the dataset, it didn't sway the outcome much here. This decision reflects how the model weighs consistent academic deciles heavily for bar success predictions. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average of 37, improving your grades to climb those school deciles, and perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or academic coaching proven to help students in similar positions pass the bar. Reapplying after strengthening these areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically in future evaluations.
